query: FAA proposed civil penalty United Airlines flight attendant drug alcohol violation

mako.raw:
{"query":"FAA proposed civil penalty United Airlines flight attendant drug alcohol violation","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1665,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.faa.gov/newsroom/faa-proposes-255000-fine-against-united-airlines-alleged-drug-and-alcohol-violation","title":"FAA Proposes $255,000 Fine Against United Airlines for Alleged Drug and Alcohol Violation | Federal Aviation Administration","content":"1 day ago — FAA Proposes $255,000 Fine Against United Airlines for Alleged Drug and Alcohol Violation. Thursday, July 16, 2026. WASHINGTON — The Federal ...Read more
The FAA alleges United allowed a flight attendant to perform safety-sensitive duties on 47 flights between May 2024 and July 2025 without the evaluation of a substance abuse professional, without a verified negative return-to-duty drug test result, and without being subject to follow-up testing.
United terminated the flight attendant in 2021 for refusal to submit to a drug test and subsequently re-hired him.
United has 30 days after receiving the FAA’s enforcement letter to respond to the agency."},{"url":"https://www.faa.gov/newsroom/faa-proposes-255000-fine-against-american-airlines-alleged-drug-and-alcohol-violation","title":"FAA Proposes $255,000 Fine Against American Airlines for Alleged Drug and Alcohol Violation | Federal Aviation Administration","content":"WASHINGTON — The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) proposes a $255,000 civil penalty against American Airlines (American) for allegedly violating drug and alcohol regulations.
The FAA alleges that between May 2019 and December 2023 American allowed 12 flight attendants who tested positive on drug and alcohol tests to resume performing safety-sensitive duties without completing all the required follow-up testing.
The employees tested positive for substances including alcohol, amphetamines, cocaine, marijuana, and methamphetamine."},{"url":"https://aerocorner.com/news/faa-united-drug-alcohol-penalty/","title":"FAA Fines United $255K Over Drug Testing | AeroCorner","content":"On July 16, 2026, the Federal Aviation Administration said it is proposing a $255,000 civil penalty against United Airlines, alleging the carrier let a flight attendant perform safety-sensitive duties on 47 flights without finishing the return-to-duty process required after a drug or alcohol violation.
According to the FAA, the flight attendant worked those 47 flights across 2024 and 2025 without first completing the steps federal rules require before an employee can return to safety-sensitive work.
The FAA also said the same employee had been dismissed by United in 2021 for refusing to take a required drug test, then was later rehired.
In U.S. aviation, anyone in a safety-sensitive role, including pilots, cabin crew, mechanics, dispatchers, and air traffic controllers, falls under federal drug and alcohol testing regulations.
They first have to complete a structured return-to-duty program: an evaluation by a substance abuse professional, any recommended treatment or education, and a verified negative test before returning.
In April 2026, the FAA proposed a matching $255,000 penalty against American Airlines and a $304,000 penalty against Southwest for comparable failures to keep crew members out of safety-sensitive duties until the return-to-duty process was complete.","date":"11 hours ago"},{"url":"https://www.faa.gov/","title":"Federal Aviation Administration","content":"The FAA will allow Boeing to resume issuing airworthiness certificates at the end of the production process for all 737 MAX and 787 airplanes."}]}
